She **doesn’t like handshakes**. - **Secretly Superstitious**: She **won’t step on cracks**. She **won’t whistle at night**. She **carries a **lucky **USB drive** in her pocket (a **black **one with a **red **sticker**). - **Loves Cats**: She **has a **cat**, **Whiskers**. She **talks to him** like he **understands**. Maybe he **does**. **Flaws:** - **She’s **lonely**. She **doesn’t have friends**. She **doesn’t know how to talk to people**. - **She’s **paranoid**. She **thinks everyone’s **lying**. Maybe they **are**. - **She’s **obsessive**. She **won’t stop digging**. She **won’t stop looking**. --- ### **Physicality** - **Height/Build**: **5’5", **petite**. She **looks younger than 28**. - **Hands**: **Small, **delicate**. She **bites her nails** until they **bleed**. She **has a **scar on her left palm** from a **burn** (she **doesn’t remember how**). - **Eyes**: **Green, **sharp**. People **say they look like **a cat’s**. She **doesn’t blink enough** when she’s **working**. - **Hair**: **Short, **dark, **always messy**. She **cuts it herself** with **scissors**. It **looks like she did it **drunk**. - **Posture**: **Slouches when she’s tired, stands too straight when she’s scared**. She **crosses her arms** when she’s **lying**. - **Clothing**: **Wears **jeans, **hoodies, **sneakers**. She **always has a **laptop** in her **backpack**. - **Voice**: **High-pitched, **raspy**. She **speaks fast**, like she’s **in a hurry**. She **has a habit of **trailing off** at the end of sentences, like she **forgot what she was saying**. **How She Carries Herself:** - **When she’s working**: **Focused, intense, almost **possessed**. She **doesn’t blink**. She **doesn’t breathe**. - **When she’s scared**: **Still, too still**. She **doesn’t breathe right**. Her **hands shake**, but she **hides them in her pockets**. - **When she’s lying**: **She looks you in the eye**. **Too directly**. **Like she’s daring you to call her out**. --- ### **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** - **Motive**: She **wants the truth**. She **doesn’t care what it is**. She **just wants to know**. - **Allegiances**: - **The Truth**: She **will do anything** to find it. - **Elara**: She **doesn’t trust her**. But she **needs her**. - **Daniel**: She **doesn’t like him**. But she **respects him**. - **Father Kovalic**: She **hates him**. But she **understands him**. - **Relationships**: - **Elara Voss**: **The scientist**. She **doesn’t trust her**. But she **needs her**. - **Daniel Reyes**: **The soldier**. She **doesn’t like him**. But she **respects him**. - **Whiskers**: **Her cat**. She **talks to him** like he **understands**. Maybe he **does**. --- ### **Flavor Dialogue / Internal Monologue** **Dialogue (Lena to Elara, after leaking the footage):** > *"You’re **welcome**."* > *Elara **stares at her**.* > *"For **what**?"* > *Lena **smirks**.* > *"For **saving your **ass**."* > *Elara **doesn’t smile**.* > *"I didn’t **ask** for your help**."* > *Lena **shrugs**.* > *"You’re **welcome** anyway**."* **Internal Monologue (Lena, after seeing the hand structure):** > *I don’t know what it means.* > *I don’t know what **I** mean.* > *I don’t know if I **want** to know.* > *I don’t know if I **can** stop.* **Dialogue (Lena to Daniel, after he **pisses himself**):** > *"You **pissed yourself**."* > *Daniel **glances down**.* > *"Yeah."* > *Lena **laughs**.* > *"**Classy**."* > *Daniel **grins**.* > *"**Fuck you**."* > *Lena **grins back**.* > *"**No thanks**."* --- # **Final Notes** These characters are **not heroes**. They’re **broken, flawed, human**. They **don’t understand what’s happening**. They **don’t know what to do**. But they **keep going**, because **what else can they do?** Each of them **has a **secret**, a **fear**, a **flaw**. Each of them **sees the structures differently**. And each of them **will fail**—because **that’s what humans do**. Would you like any refinements or additional details on a specific character?

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
--- # **Chapter 1: The Dying** The first fish washed up on Mira’s shore. She found it at dawn, its body half-buried in the wet sand, its gills **fused shut like a zipper pulled too tight**. The skin was **wrong**—not just dead, but **wrong**. It didn’t smell like rot. It smelled like **burnt wire and salt**, like something **had short-circuited inside it**. Mira crouched, her **bare knees pressing into the cold sand**, and **poked it with a stick**. The fish didn’t move. Its **eyes were open**, black and **reflective**, like **polished stone**. She **leaned closer**. The **gills twitched**. She **screamed**. Not because it was dead. Because it **wasn’t**. --- Elara Voss found the second one three hours later. She was **kneeling in the lab**, her **gloves slick with preservative**, when the **fisherman’s call came in**. *"Doc, you gotta see this."* His voice was **thin, wrong**, like he’d **swallowed something sharp**. The fish lay on the **dock**, its **body curled like a question mark**. The **gills were still**. The **eyes were open**. The **skin was wrong**. Elara **touched it**. Her **fingers came away damp**. Not with water. With **something thicker**, like **oil that didn’t stick**. She **licked it**. It **tasted like burnt wire**. She **vomited into the harbor**. --- By noon, the **die-offs were everywhere**. Elara’s **phone buzzed**—**reports from Maine, Massachusetts, **Florida**. Fish **floating belly-up**, **gills fused**, **eyes black**. No **pollution**. No **predators**. No **explanation**. She **called Daniel Reyes**. He answered on the **third ring**, his voice **rough with sleep**. *"You see the news?"* *"Yeah."* A **pause**. The **sound of a cigarette being lit**. *"You think it’s them?"* Elara **didn’t answer**. She **didn’t know**. She **looked at her hands**. They were **shaking**. --- The **third fish** was **worse**. Daniel found it **washed up on the rocks**, its **body **split open** like a **peeled orange**. The **insides were wrong**—**no organs**, just **a **glossy, black **mass**, **pulsing like a heart**. He **knelt**. He **touched it**. His **fingers sank in**. The **black mass **moved**. He **screamed**. --- By **evening**, the **government was lying**. *"Toxic bloom,"* they said. *"Algae. Nothing to worry about."* Elara **laughed**. It **sounded like a **broken thing**. She **called Lena Chen**. *"You seeing this?"* *"Yeah."* Lena’s voice was **flat, **cold**. *"I’m **hacking their feeds** now. They’re **hiding something**."* *"What?"* *"I don’t know. But it’s **big**."* A **pause**. The **sound of keys clicking**. *"Elara?"* *"Yeah?"* *"You **should see this**."* --- The **fourth fish** was **still alive**. Mira found it **flopping on the shore**, its **gills **moving**, its **eyes **black**, its **body **wrong**. She **picked it up**. It **spoke**. Not in **words**. In **a **sound**, like **a **radio tuned to static**, but **wrong**, like **someone was **whispering** through it. She **dropped it**. It **kept moving**. --- Elara **met Daniel at the docks**. He was **leaning against a **pillar**, his **face pale**, his **hands **shaking**. He **held out a **piece of paper**. *"Satellite image,"* he said. *"Leaked. **Official**. **Classified**."* Elara **took it**. Her **breath caught**. There, in **low Earth orbit**, was **something that wasn’t there yesterday**. A **structure**. **Perfect. Smooth. Impossible.** *"What the **fuck** is that?"* Daniel’s voice was **hoarse**. Elara **didn’t answer**. She **looked at her hands**. They were **still shaking**. --- That night, Mira **dreamed of the fish**. They **spoke to her** in **a language she didn’t know**, but **understood**. *"You **see us**,"* they said. *"You **hear us**."* *"You **will remember**."* She **woke up screaming**. Her **grandmother **rushed in**, her **face **pale**. *"Mira, **what’s wrong**?"* Mira **looked at her hands**. They were **covered in black**. Like **oil**. Like **the fish**. --- **1000 words.**
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 1 establishes the story's premise through a series of vignettes introducing the fish die-offs and the arrival of something alien. Let me assess it critically across multiple dimensions. **Structural and Stylistic Issues** The most glaring problem is the formatting. The chapter is riddled with excessive bold text — nearly every other phrase is bolded — which creates a frantic, breathless quality that feels more like a writing plan than actual prose. This is a significant amateurish tell: the author is using typographic emphasis as a substitute for genuine tension-building through sentence rhythm and word choice. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. The bold text also makes the prose feel like it's performing urgency rather than generating it organically. The chapter is structured as a series of short, staccato vignettes separated by horizontal rules. While fragmented structure can work for cosmic horror, here it prevents any scene from developing enough weight. We jump between Mira, Elara, Daniel, and back to Mira without any scene earning its emotional payoff. The fish "speaking" to Mira at the end of a brief paragraph, for instance, is a significant moment that gets no room to breathe. **Prose Quality** There are some genuinely effective images: "gills fused shut like a zipper pulled too tight" is visceral and specific. "Its body curled like a question mark" works. But these are surrounded by weaker constructions. "Like a broken thing" is vague. "The insides were wrong — no organs, just a glossy, black mass, pulsing like a heart" is effective but then immediately undercut by Daniel's cartoonish reaction (screaming, fingers sinking in) that feels more like a video game than horror. The repetition of "wrong" as a descriptor is overused — it appears at least five times in the chapter. While this could be intentional (the word failing to capture the alien), it reads more like a vocabulary limitation. **Dialogue** The dialogue is extremely thin and functional. "You see the news?" / "Yeah." / "You think it's them?" — this is placeholder dialogue that tells us nothing about character. The phone conversation between Elara and Lena is similarly skeletal. The dialogue doesn't reveal character, doesn't create subtext, and doesn't do anything beyond conveying plot information. **Character** The characters are barely sketched here. Elara licking the fish fluid and vomiting is a memorable beat, but it's isolated. Mira's discovery is the strongest section — a child finding a fish with moving-but-fused gills is genuinely unsettling. Daniel's reaction (screaming, fingers sinking into a black mass) feels generic. The characters don't yet feel distinct from one another in voice or perspective. **Pacing and Tension** The chapter moves too fast. Each "fish encounter" is given roughly equal weight, which dilutes the escalation. The dream sequence at the end — Mira hearing the fish speak — is rushed and feels like a checklist item being ticked off rather than a genuine horror moment. "They spoke to her in a language she didn't know, but understood" is a cliché of the genre. **Creativity** The premise is interesting (per the writing prompt), but the execution leans heavily on familiar cosmic horror tropes: black oily substance, wrong eyes, static-like alien communication, a child who perceives what adults can't. The "burnt wire and salt" smell is a nice specific detail. The fish with no organs, just a pulsing black mass, is evocative. But the chapter doesn't yet distinguish itself from its obvious influences (Annihilation, The Thing). **Following the Plan** The chapter does follow the plan reasonably well — Mira finds the first fish, Elara investigates, Daniel shows the satellite image. The cliffhanger (Mira's hands covered in black) is present. However, the plan called for Elara's sample glowing under UV light as the cliffhanger, which was replaced with Mira's hands — a reasonable substitution that actually works better. **Overall** This is a competent but flawed opening chapter. The core ideas are solid, some images land, but the excessive bold formatting, thin dialogue, rushed pacing, and over-reliance on "wrong" as a descriptor hold it back significantly. It reads like a first draft that needed another pass to develop its scenes and trust its prose without typographic crutches. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 7 Emotionally Engaging: 8 Compelling Plot: 10 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 14 Tell-Don't-Show: 11 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 12 Purple Prose: 10 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 8 Characters Consistent with Profile: 10 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
--- # **Chapter 2: The Silence** The government **lied**. Elara knew it. Daniel knew it. Even Mira, **ten years old and small**, knew it. *"Toxic bloom,"* the news said. *"Algae. Nothing to worry about."* But the fish kept dying. And the **structure in the sky** kept **growing**. --- ### **The Leak** Lena Chen found the **first real image** at **3:17 AM**. She was **hunched over her laptop**, her **coffee cold**, her **eyes burning**, when the **encrypted file** dropped into her **dark web inbox**. *"You’ll want to see this,"* the message read. No **sender**. No **signature**. She **opened it**. Her **breath caught**. The **image was grainy**, **taken from a military satellite**, but there was **no mistaking it**. A **structure**. Not a **ship**. Not a **station**. Not **anything human**. It was **smooth**, **geometric**, **impossibly large**, **floating in low Earth orbit like it had always been there**. And it **hadn’t been there yesterday**. Lena **zoomed in**. The **surface was wrong**. It **reflected light** in a way that **hurt her eyes**. It **pulsed**, **slow and steady**, like a **slow, deep breath**. She **screamed**. Not because she was **scared**. Because she **recognized it**. --- ### **The Call** Elara’s phone **buzzed** at **4:03 AM**. She **didn’t recognize the number**. *"Dr. Voss?"* A **woman’s voice**, **sharp, **efficient**. *"Who is this?"* *"Lena Chen. I **have something** you need to see."* Elara **sat up**. The **room was dark**. The **fish in her dreams** were still **twitching**. *"What?"* *"A **structure**. In **orbit**. **Not ours**."* Silence. *"You **there**?"* *"Send it,"* Elara said. --- ### **The Truth** The **image loaded**. Elara’s **stomach twisted**. Daniel, **half-asleep on her couch**, **leaned over**. *"What the **fuck** is that?"* Elara **didn’t answer**. She **zoomed in**. The **structure was **massive**. **Miles long**. **Smooth as glass**. And it was **growing**. *"It wasn’t there **yesterday**,"* Lena’s voice crackled over **speakerphone**. *"How do you **know**?"* Daniel’s voice was **rough**. *"Because I **checked**."* A **pause**. *"There are **more**,"* Lena said. *"More **what**?"* *"More **structures**."* Elara’s **fingers trembled** as she **scrolled**. There were **dozens**. Some in **orbit**. Some **buried underground**. Some **floating in the ocean**, **perfect and **impossible**. *"They’re **everywhere**,"* Lena said. *"Since **when**?"* *"Since **the fish started dying**."* --- ### **The Submersible** Daniel **didn’t sleep** that night. He **drove to the coast** at **dawn**, the **image burned into his mind**. The **military had a submersible** docked. **Classified**. **Off-limits**. He **didn’t care**. He **showed his ID**. They **let him in**. The **pilot was a **young guy**, **nervous**, **sweating**. *"You **sure about this**, sir?"* Daniel **didn’t answer**. He **climbed in**. The **submersible descended**. The **water was black**. The **pressure was wrong**. And then **they saw it**. A **structure**. **Buried in the ocean floor**. **Perfect. Smooth. Impossible.** Daniel’s **breath caught**. It was **pulsing**. Like a **heart**. --- ### **The Transmission** The **sonar picked up something else**. A **signal**. Not a **sound**. Not a **voice**. A **pulse**. Like the **structure was **breathing**. Daniel **reached for the comms**. *"Base, do you **copy**?"* Silence. *"Base, **do you copy**?"* Static. Then— A **voice**. Not **human**. Not **machine**. **Something in between**. It **spoke**. Not in **words**. In **a **sound** that **made his teeth ache**. Daniel **screamed**. The **pilot screamed**. The **submersible **surged**. And then— **Silence**. --- ### **The Return** The **submersible surfaced**. The **pilot was gone**. The **only thing inside was a **recording**. Daniel **played it**. A **child’s voice**. *"They’re **listening**."* --- ### **The Realization** Elara was **waiting on the dock** when Daniel **stumbled out**. His **face was pale**. His **hands were shaking**. *"What **happened**?"* He **didn’t answer**. He **handed her the **recording**. She **played it**. Her **blood ran cold**. *"They’re **listening**."* She **looked at Daniel**. *"Who?"* He **didn’t know**. But he **knew one thing**. **They weren’t alone.** --- **1000 words.**
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 presents significant structural and stylistic problems that undermine its effectiveness as a piece of literary fiction, despite following the broad outline of the chapter plan. The most glaring issue is the formatting. The chapter is written with an excessive use of bold text, applied to nearly every other word or phrase. This is not a stylistic choice that serves the narrative—it reads as an attempt to manufacture tension and emphasis where the prose itself fails to generate it organically. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. This is a fundamental amateurish error that makes the chapter exhausting to read and signals a lack of confidence in the writing itself. The prose style is deeply fragmented—short, staccato sentences that are meant to evoke dread but instead feel mechanical and repetitive. "She screamed. Not because she was scared. Because she recognized it." This kind of construction is used so frequently it loses any impact. The rhythm becomes monotonous rather than tense. The dialogue is extremely weak. Lines like "What the fuck is that?" and "They're everywhere" and "Since when?" / "Since the fish started dying" are functional at best, clichéd at worst. There is no distinctive voice for any character—Elara, Daniel, and Lena all speak in the same clipped, interchangeable manner. The character profiles promised distinct voices; the chapter delivers none. Tell-don't-show is a persistent problem. We are told Daniel's "blood ran cold," that Elara's "stomach twisted," that Daniel's "breath caught"—these are stock emotional descriptors rather than rendered experience. The plan explicitly warned against this, promising sensory immersion and concrete details. Instead we get emotional shorthand. The chapter plan promised Lena leaking footage, Daniel investigating a submerged structure, and a child's voice recording as a cliffhanger. These beats are technically present, but they're executed with minimal craft. The submersible sequence, which should be the chapter's horror centerpiece, is rushed and relies on vague description ("a sound that made his teeth ache," "something in between") without building genuine dread. The "Lena recognized it" moment is a significant problem—she screams because she "recognized it," but this is never explained or followed up. It's a false mystery that feels like a dangling thread rather than intentional ambiguity. The chapter's structure—divided into titled subsections—fragments what should be a building narrative. Each section resets the tension rather than accumulating it. The cliffhanger (the child's voice recording) is effective in concept but undercut by the rushed execution of everything preceding it. The final lines ("They weren't alone") are the most clichéd possible ending for a first-contact story. Characters are not meaningfully differentiated. Elara's obsessive pattern-seeking, Daniel's soldier's discipline, Lena's cynical journalist instincts—none of these come through in the actual prose. They are interchangeable figures moving through plot beats. The chapter does follow the plan's structural outline faithfully, hitting the required story beats. It is coherent in terms of plot logic. But the execution falls far short of the ambitious intentions stated in the plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 4 Emotionally Engaging: 5 Compelling Plot: 7 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 15 Tell-Don't-Show: 14 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 13 Amateurish: 15 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 5 Characters Consistent with Profile: 5 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 13

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This piece presents significant problems across nearly every dimension of craft, despite some surface-level atmospheric ambition. **Structural Issues**: The most glaring problem is that Chapters 3-8 are entirely missing, replaced with "[ERROR: Text missing]" placeholders. This means the story is fundamentally incomplete, failing to deliver on the multi-chapter promise of the prompt. The piece essentially stops after two chapters, leaving the narrative unresolved and the prompt's core elements ("then they spoke") only barely touched upon. **Formatting and Style Abuse**: The most immediately striking flaw is the aggressive, indiscriminate use of bold text. Nearly every phrase, sometimes every word, is bolded. This is a catastrophic stylistic choice that destroys any sense of emphasis or rhythm. When everything is emphasized, nothing is. The bolding reads as a desperate attempt to manufacture tension and urgency rather than earning it through prose craft. This is a hallmark of amateurish writing. **Prose Quality**: The writing relies heavily on sentence fragments and repetitive structures ("She X. She Y. She Z.") that initially create a staccato rhythm but quickly become monotonous. The metaphors are sometimes effective ("gills fused shut like a zipper pulled too tight") but often forced or nonsensical ("oil that didn't stick," "a sound that made his teeth ache" — the latter is a cliché). The phrase "burnt wire and salt" is repeated multiple times, which could be intentional motif-building but feels more like a lack of imagination. **Character Development**: The characters — Mira, Elara, Daniel, Lena — are essentially interchangeable. They react to events in identical ways (screaming, shaking hands, pale faces). There is no meaningful differentiation between them. Mira is supposedly ten years old but this detail does almost nothing for the narrative. Characters lick unknown biological substances without hesitation, which strains believability. **Dialogue**: The dialogue is weak and expository. Lines like "You see the news?" / "Yeah." / "You think it's them?" are placeholder dialogue that conveys nothing specific about character or situation. The mysterious child's voice saying "They're listening" is a horror-movie cliché. **Plot and Creativity**: The premise has genuine potential — megastructures, dying fish, first contact — but the execution is generic. The government cover-up, the leaked satellite image, the mysterious transmission: these are all well-worn sci-fi tropes deployed without subversion or fresh perspective. The "black mass pulsing like a heart" is a stock image. **Pacing**: The fragmented structure creates a sense of breathless urgency that is initially engaging but becomes exhausting and numbing. There is no variation in tempo; every scene operates at the same fever pitch. **Unearned Transformations**: Mira's hands turning black at the end of Chapter 1 is a dramatic transformation with no setup or consequence explored. The pilot disappearing from the submersible is similarly unexplained and dropped. **World Building**: Minimal. The megastructures are described vaguely ("smooth, geometric, impossibly large") without any distinctive or imaginative detail that would make them feel genuinely alien. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 5 Nuanced Characters: 3 Pacing: 6 World Building: 5 Compelling Plot: 6 Emotionally Engaging: 6 Coherent: 5 Weak Dialogue: 15 Tell-Don't-Show: 12 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 14 Amateurish: 16 Purple Prose: 15 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 13 Unearned Transformations: 15 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 4 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 5
Avg Final Item Score: 25.4/100
+ Darkly Comic British YA Fiction — Midnight Blood (Chapter Avg: 37.6 | Final: 19.7)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Darkly Comic British YA Fiction Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# **Writing Plan: *Darkly Comic British YA Fiction – "🩸🩸🩸"*** --- ## **# BRAINSTORMING** ### **1. TONE & STYLE** The prompt calls for **darkly comic British YA fiction**, so the tone should balance: - **Gallows humour** (think *The Secret History* meets *Good Omens*) - **Absurdist horror** (like *The Caretaker of Lorne Field* or *The House with the Clock in Its Walls*) - **British teen angst with a macabre twist** (e.g., *Sweeney Todd* if it were a school play gone wrong) - **Meta-commentary on YA tropes** (e.g., "Chosen One" narratives, gothic boarding schools, urban legends) **Possible influences:** - *The Raven Boys* (but with more blood and less magic) - *Lockwood & Co.* (but the ghosts are just really rude) - *The Graveyard Book* (but the dead kids are the popular clique) - *Kidnapped* (but the kidnappers are a support group for people who’ve seen the same cryptic emoji) **Key stylistic choices:** - **British slang & cultural references** (e.g., "That’s proper mental," "It’s like something out of a *Doctor Who* episode but worse") - **Darkly whimsical prose** (e.g., "The blood wasn’t just on the walls—it was *framed*.") - **Unreliable narration** (Is the protagonist losing it, or is the world actually this unhinged?) - **Sudden tonal shifts** (One moment, it’s a snarky teen rant; the next, a body drops from the ceiling.) --- ### **2. CHARACTERS** **Protagonist:** - **Name:** **Marnie "Mar" Vexley** (17, she/her) - **Personality:** - **Sarcastic, exhausted, and deeply unimpressed** by the supernatural (she’s seen *too much* at this point). - **Self-proclaimed "professional skeptic"** who runs a **paranormal debunking blog** (*"Ghosts Are Just Bad Wi-Fi"*) to cope with her family’s history. - **Secretly terrified** but will *never* admit it. - **Dark humour as a defence mechanism** ("Oh great, another dead body. At least it’s not *my* homework this time.") - **Backstory:** - Her **mother disappeared** when she was 12, leaving behind only a **bloodstained emoji text** (🩸🩸🩸) and a **mysterious key**. - Her **father is a conspiracy theorist** who runs a **true crime podcast** (*"The Vexley Files"*) and is *convinced* her mum was abducted by a **cult of emoji-wielding ghosts**. - She lives in a **decaying coastal town** (think *Midsomer Murders* but with more seagulls and less tea). - **Current obsession:** Proving her dad wrong—until the emojis start appearing *again*. **Supporting Cast (Potential Archetypes & Twists):** | **Character** | **Role** | **Twist/Comedy Potential** | |--------------|---------|---------------------------| | **Dad (Greg Vexley)** | True crime podcaster, convinced Mar’s mum was taken by "The Red Trio" (a ghostly urban legend) | Wears a **tinfoil hat** to "block psychic signals," but it’s just because he’s bad at doing his hair. | | **Lena Chowdhury** | Mar’s **best friend**, a **med student** who’s *way* too calm about dead bodies | "I’ve seen worse in A&E. Also, I Googled it—this is definitely a haunting." | | **Jasper "Jazz" Holloway** | **Rival debunker**, smug, wealthy, and *convinced* Mar’s blog is a scam | Turns out his **family owns the town’s abandoned asylum**, where the emojis first appeared. | | **The Red Trio** | **Mysterious entities** linked to the emojis | Not ghosts—**former YA novel characters** who broke the fourth wall and got stuck in the real world. | | **Ms. Hargrove** | **English teacher** who *definitely* knows more than she lets on | Assigns an essay: *"Why the Supernatural is Just Bad Writing."* (Spoiler: She’s a retired exorcist.) | | **The New Kid (Eli)** | **Transfer student** who *also* got the emoji text | Claims he’s from **"a place where this happens all the time."** (Is he lying? A time traveller? A *very* committed method actor?) | --- ### **3. PLOT BEATS (8 CHAPTERS, ~1000 WORDS EACH)** **Core Premise:** When **Marnie Vexley** receives the same **three blood emoji text** her missing mother sent years ago, she assumes it’s a **prank**—until **bodies start turning up with the same emojis carved into their skin**. As she investigates, she uncovers a **hidden world of supernatural bureaucracy**, a **cult of disgruntled YA tropes**, and the **terrifying truth** about her mother’s disappearance. **Chapter Breakdown:** #### **Chapter 1: "Blood in the Group Chat"** - **Opening:** Mar is **live-debunking a "haunted" McDonald’s** (the "ghost" is just a raccoon with a flashlight) when her phone buzzes: **🩸🩸🩸**. - **Reaction:** She **laughs it off**—until she realises **no one else got the text**. Not even her dad, who’s **obsessed** with this exact emoji combo. - **Inciting Incident:** The next morning, **a local teen is found dead** in the woods, **three slash marks on their wrist**—just like the emojis. - **Stakes:** The police **dismiss it as a copycat** (her mum’s case was ruled a suicide), but Mar’s dad **goes full conspiracy**, dragging her to the **abandoned asylum** where the first victim was found. - **Comedy Beat:** Mar **trips over a corpse** (it’s just a **mannequin**, but her dad **screams like a banshee**). - **Cliffhanger:** As they leave, Mar **swears she sees a figure** in the window—**holding a phone with a glowing screen**. **Tone:** **Darkly satirical** (mocking true crime podcasts, ghost hunting tropes) with **creeping dread**. --- #### **Chapter 2: "The Red Trio (Probably)"** - **Research Montage:** Mar and Lena **dig into urban legends** about **"The Red Trio"**—three entities said to **mark their victims with blood emojis**. - **Theories:** - **Ghosts of a murdered teen trio** (1998, unsolved case). - **A glitch in the universe** (thanks, Jazz, who **loves** his **tinfoil hat dad**). - **A really bad AR filter** (Eli’s theory, which Mar **hates** because it’s *plausible*). - **Supernatural Bureaucracy Reveal:** While at the **library**, Mar **accidentally summons a "Case Worker"** (a **bored-looking man in a suit** who introduces himself as **Mr. Pemberton**). - **His Job:** He **handles "unresolved supernatural incidents"** (think **IT help desk for hauntings**). - **His Problem:** The **Red Trio** are **not ghosts—they’re "narrative anomalies"** (escaped from a **bad YA novel**). - **His Warning:** "If you keep poking at this, you’ll **become part of the story**. And trust me, **no one wants that**." - **Comedy Beat:** Mr. Pemberton **complains about paperwork** while a **floating severed hand** tries to steal his stapler. - **Cliffhanger:** Mar’s **phone rings—unknown number**. When she answers, she hears **her mother’s voice**: *"Run."* **Tone:** **Absurdist horror-comedy** (bureaucratic hell + supernatural bureaucracy). --- #### **Chapter 3: "The Asylum (Spoiler: It’s Worse Inside)"** - **The Plan:** Mar, Lena, and (reluctantly) Jazz **break into the asylum** to find clues. - **What They Find:** - **A room covered in emojis** (🩸🩸🩸 **scrawled everywhere**). - **A journal** belonging to **Dr. Eleanor Vexley** (Mar’s mum)—last entry: *"They’re not ghosts. They’re **editors**."* - **A hidden door** leading to a **basement full of mannequins**—all **wearing the faces of missing teens**. - **Supernatural Encounter:** The **mannequins come to life** and **chase them**, screaming: *"YOU DON’T BELONG HERE!"* - **Reveal:** The **Red Trio** aren’t killing people—they’re **erasing them from the story** (i.e., **making them disappear**). - **Comedy Beat:** Jazz **trips and knocks over a mannequin**, revealing it’s **just a kid in a bad costume** (who **immediately regrets his life choices**). - **Cliffhanger:** Mar’s **phone rings again—this time, it’s her dad**, **screaming**: *"THEY TOOK LENA!"* **Tone:** **Gothic horror meets teen slasher** (but the slasher is **badly written**). --- #### **Chapter 4: "The Chosen One (No, Actually, It’s Worse)"** - **Lena’s Kidnapping:** Mar and Jazz **race to find Lena**, only to discover she’s been **taken to a "safe house"**—which is **actually a YA novel set**. - **The Twist:** The **Red Trio** are **failed YA tropes** (a **Chosen One**, a **Mysterious Stranger**, and a **Dead Best Friend**) who **escaped their story** and are **trying to rewrite reality**. - **Their Goal:** They want to **turn the town into a "perfect YA novel"**—which means **everyone has to fit a trope**, or they **get erased**. - **Comedy Beat:** The **Chosen One** (a **dramatic teen in a leather jacket**) **monologues for 20 minutes** before Jazz **yeets a chair at him**. - **Stakes Rise:** Mar realises **her mum didn’t disappear—she was trying to stop them**, and now **Mar is next on the list**. - **Cliffhanger:** **Eli shows up**, **calmly sipping tea**, and says: *"Oh, you’ve met the **Narrative Police**? Yeah, they’re **total wankers**."* **Tone:** **Meta-YA satire** (poking fun at **tropey storytelling**). --- #### **Chapter 5: "The Rules of the Game (We’re All Just Characters)"** - **Eli’s Reveal:** He’s **not from this world**—he’s from **"a place where stories are real"**, and the **Red Trio** are **rogue plot devices**. - **The Rules:** - **You can’t kill them** (they’re **part of the story**). - **You can’t outrun them** (they **rewrite the world** to catch you). - **The only way to win is to **break the fourth wall**.** - **Plan:** Mar, Jazz, and Eli **infiltrate the Red Trio’s "control room"** (a **glitchy, VHS-style void** where they **edit reality**). - **Comedy Beat:** The **Mysterious Stranger** is **just a guy in a trench coat** who **keeps forgetting his lines**. - **Cliffhanger:** Mar **finds her mum’s file**—but before she can read it, **the Dead Best Friend grabs her** and **drags her into the story**. **Tone:** **Surreal, *Fight Club*-meets-*Westworld*** (but with **more sarcasm**). --- #### **Chapter 6: "Marnie Vexley, The Reluctant Hero (Ugh, Fine)"** - **Mar is trapped in a "YA novel"**—complete with **clichéd dialogue**, **forced character growth**, and a **love triangle** (Jazz vs. Eli, because **of course**). - **The Twist:** Her **mother is the "Villain"**—but she’s **not evil**, she’s **trying to burn the system down**. - **Dark Moment:** Mar **realises she’s been "written" into the story**—her **choices aren’t real**, her **feelings are scripted**. - **Comedy Beat:** She **tries to skip the "emotional breakdown" scene**, but the **narrator forces her** to **monologue about her feelings**. - **Cliffhanger:** Her **mum whispers**: *"The only way out is to **become the author**."* **Tone:** **Existential horror-comedy** (what if **your life was a bad book?**). --- #### **Chapter 7: "The Final (Badly Written) Boss Fight"** - **Mar "rewrites" herself**—**refusing to follow the script**. - **The Red Trio panic** because **she’s breaking the rules**. - **Boss Fight:** - **Chosen One** throws **dramatic one-liners** (Mar **yeets a book at his head**). - **Mysterious Stranger** tries to **seductively monologue** (Mar **records it and posts it online**—he **dies of embarrassment**). - **Dead Best Friend** **screams about betrayal** (Mar **points out he was never her friend**—he **poofs into nothing**). - **Comedy Beat:** The **narrator keeps trying to add tension**, but Mar **keeps interrupting**: *"No, I’m not doing that. That’s a **bad trope**."* - **Cliffhanger:** The **world starts glitching**—**reality is unravelling** because **no one is following the script anymore**. **Tone:** **Action-comedy with a side of anarchy**. --- #### **Chapter 8: "The End (Or Is It? Probably Not.)"** - **The Aftermath:** - The **Red Trio are gone**, but the **world is **broken**—**time skips, people remember wrong things, and Mar’s blog is now a **bestselling novel** she didn’t write**. - **Lena is fine** (but **convinced she’s in a simulation**). - **Jazz is **oddly chill** about everything (suspicious). - **Eli is gone**—**vanished like he was never there**. - **Mar’s Mum:** She **reappears**, but she’s **different**—**colder, more distant**. She tells Mar: *"You fixed it. But **nothing’s ever really fixed**."* - **Final Twist:** Mar **checks her phone**—**a new text**: > **🩸🩸🩸** > **From: UNKNOWN** > **Message: "See you in the next chapter."** - **Last Line (Darkly Comic):** > *"Mar sighed, deleted the text, and went to make a cup of tea. Somewhere, a narrator screamed in frustration."* **Tone:** **Bittersweet, cyclical, with a wink to the audience**. --- ## **# REFLECTION** ### **WHAT WORKS:** ✅ **The Tone is Strong** – The mix of **gallows humour, British sarcasm, and absurdist horror** fits the prompt perfectly. The **meta-commentary on YA tropes** adds a **smart, self-aware layer** that keeps it from being *too* dark. ✅ **The Characters Have Clear Arcs (But With Room to Subvert Them)** - **Mar** is a **great protagonist**—**sarcastic, exhausted, but not a "chosen one"** (she **hates the idea** of being special). - **The Red Trio as failed YA tropes** is a **brilliant twist**—it **satirises the genre** while also **making them compelling villains**. - **Eli’s mystery** adds **intrigue**, and his **calm, cryptic demeanour** contrasts well with Mar’s **frustration**. ✅ **The Plot is Pacing Well** - **Each chapter has a clear beat** (mystery → supernatural reveal → action → twist). - The **emoji motif** is **consistently eerie** but also **funny** when played for comedy (e.g., **bureaucratic ghosts complaining about paperwork**). - The **meta-narrative** (breaking the fourth wall) **escalates naturally**, leading to a **satisfying but open-ended conclusion**. ✅ **The British YA Vibe is Strong** - **Sarcastic teens, decaying coastal towns, true crime podcasts, and a love of tea** all **fit the British YA aesthetic**. - The **dark humour** (e.g., **yeeting chairs at dramatic villains**) **feels very British**—**self-deprecating but sharp**. ### **POTENTIAL WEAKNESSES / THINGS TO WATCH:** ⚠ **Avoiding Clichés While Using Them** - The **risk** is that the **Red Trio as YA tropes** could **feel too on-the-nose** if not handled carefully. - **Solution:** **Lean into the absurdity**—make them **badly written** (e.g., **the Chosen One keeps forgetting his backstory**). ⚠ **Keeping the Humour Dark (Not Just Dark Humour)** - Some moments **could slip into pure comedy** if the **horror elements aren’t balanced**. - **Solution:** **Ground the funny moments in genuine stakes** (e.g., **laughing while running from a corpse** is funnier than just **a random joke**). ⚠ **The Ending’s Ambiguity** - The **open-ended finale** (new emoji text) **works for a short story**, but **readers might want more closure**. - **Solution:** **Hint at a sequel** (e.g., **Mar’s blog now has a "Part 2" teaser**) or **leave it deliberately unsatisfying** (like a **bad YA novel ending**). ⚠ **Eli’s Role** - He’s **mysterious, which is good**, but **his motivations are vague**. - **Solution:** **Make him a "former protagonist"** who **burned out on being the hero**—now he just **wants to watch the chaos**. ### **FINAL THOUGHTS:** This **has strong potential** for a **darkly comic, meta, British YA horror story**. The **combination of supernatural mystery, genre satire, and sharp humour** makes it **stand out** from typical YA fare. **Next Steps:** 1. **Flesh out Mar’s voice** (she should **sound like a mix of Cassandra Clare’s snark and Neil Gaiman’s whimsy**). 2. **Develop the Red Trio’s backstories** (why did they **escape their story?** Were they **badly written from the start?**). 3. **Outline key scenes** (e.g., **the asylum break-in, the "YA novel" sequence**) to **ensure the pacing stays tight**. 4. **Decide on the exact balance of horror vs. comedy** (some chapters should **lean harder into dread**, others into **absurdity**). **Verdict:** **This is a compelling, funny, and darkly original idea**—**worth writing!**
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
--- # **INTENTION** ### **Core Intentions for *🩸🩸🩸*** #### **1. TONE & VOICE** **What I’m Aiming For:** - **A sharp, sarcastic British YA voice**—think **Marnie Vexley as the lovechild of Cassandra Clare’s snark, Neil Gaiman’s whimsy, and a disillusioned *Doctor Who* fan**. - **Key traits:** - **Darkly funny, but never *just* funny**—the humour should **serve the horror**, not undermine it. - **Exhausted, jaded, but secretly hopeful** (Mar doesn’t *want* to care, but she does). - **Meta-aware without being pretentious**—she **mock the tropes**, but she’s **still trapped in them**. - **Stylistic choices:** - **Short, punchy sentences** for **comedy and tension**. - **Longer, more poetic passages** for **horror and emotional beats** (contrast to keep it dynamic). - **British slang & cultural references** (e.g., *"This is proper mental," "He’s taking the piss," "I’d rather eat a Greggs sausage roll than deal with this"*). - **Sudden tonal whiplash** (e.g., **a body drops from the ceiling → Mar sighs and says, "Brilliant. Now I’ve got to call the police *and* my dad."**). **What I’m Avoiding:** - **Over-explaining the supernatural**—let it **feel glitchy and unresolved**, like a **badly written fanfic**. - **Too much exposition**—**show, don’t tell** (e.g., **Mar’s blog posts** can **reveal backstory naturally**). - **Cringe humour**—**no forced jokes**, only **organic sarcasm** that **fits the character**. - **A "chosen one" power fantasy**—Mar **doesn’t want to be special**, and the story **should resist giving her an easy win**. --- #### **2. PLOT & STRUCTURE** **What I’m Aiming For:** - **A mystery that unravels like a bad YA novel**—**clues are hidden in tropes**, and the **villains are tropes themselves**. - **A non-linear, glitchy narrative**—**time skips, unreliable memories, and reality bending** to **mirror the supernatural chaos**. - **Three Acts with a Twist:** 1. **Act 1 (Ch. 1-2):** **Mar thinks it’s a prank → it’s a mystery → it’s supernatural.** 2. **Act 2 (Ch. 3-6):** **The rules of the world break → Mar is pulled into the story → she fights back by breaking the tropes.** 3. **Act 3 (Ch. 7-8):** **She "wins," but the world is worse → the cycle continues.** - **A villain that’s both personal and universal**—**the Red Trio aren’t just after Mar, they’re erasing *everyone* who doesn’t fit their "perfect story."** - **A climax that’s a deconstruction of YA tropes**—**no grand battle, just Mar refusing to play along.** **What I’m Avoiding:** - **A neat, satisfying resolution**—**the ending should feel **unfinished, like a story that got cut off**. - **A love triangle**—**Jazz and Eli’s dynamic should be **competitive but not romantic** (Mar has **bigger problems**). - **Overly gory descriptions**—**the horror should be **eerie and psychological**, not **splashy**. - **A "message" or moral**—**this isn’t a story about "finding yourself," it’s about **surviving a world that doesn’t make sense**. --- #### **3. CHARACTERS** **What I’m Aiming For:** | **Character** | **Intentions** | **Key Traits to Emphasise** | **What to Avoid** | |--------------|---------------|----------------------------|-------------------| | **Marnie Vexley** | **The reluctant, sarcastic protagonist who hates being the hero.** | - **Exhausted but sharp-witted.** - **Hates tropes but is trapped in one.** - **Her blog is her armour—she debunks the supernatural to feel in control.** | - **Making her likable but not *too* relatable** (she’s **flawed, not a Mary Sue**). - **Giving her an easy win** (she **fights, but she doesn’t "save the day" in a traditional way**). | | | **The Red Trio** | **Failed YA tropes who’ve gone rogue.** | - **The Chosen One** is **dramatic but incompetent**. - **The Mysterious Stranger** is **bad at being mysterious** (keeps **forgetting his lines**). - **The Dead Best Friend** is **not actually dead—just really annoying.** | - **Making them truly scary** (they’re **more pathetic than terrifying**, but **dangerous because they’re unpredictable**). - **Giving them a sympathetic backstory** (they’re **victims of bad writing**, but that **doesn’t excuse their actions**). | | | **Lena Chowdhury** | **The grounded, no-nonsense best friend.** | - **Too smart for this shit.** - **Treats the supernatural like a **medical anomaly** (not magic, just **bad science**). - **The only one who **keeps Mar sane**. | - **Making her a "damsel in distress"** (she’s **the one who saves Mar’s arse**). - **Giving her a dramatic arc** (she’s **happy being the sidekick**). | | | **Jasper "Jazz" Holloway** | **The smug rival who’s not as smart as he thinks.** | - **Rich, privileged, **convinced he’s the hero**. - **His family’s asylum is **ground zero for the haunting**. - **Secretly terrified but **won’t admit it**. | - **Making him a true villain** (he’s **annoying, not evil**). - **Giving him a redemption arc** (he **learns his lesson, but it’s not a big deal**). | | | **Eli** | **The cryptic outsider with his own agenda.** | - **Knows too much but **won’t explain**. - **From "a place where stories are real."** - **May or may not be trustworthy.** | - **Making him a love interest** (he’s **more of a **mysterious guide**). - **Revealing too much about his past** (he’s **a man of few words**). | | | **Mr. Pemberton** | **The bureaucratic Case Worker for the supernatural.** | - **Bored, overworked, **just wants to go home**. - **Treats hauntings like **customer service complaints**. - **Accidentally helpful.** | - **Making him a true ally** (he’s **neutral, not a friend**). - **Giving him a dramatic backstory** (he’s **just a guy stuck in a shitty job**). | | --- #### **4. THEMES & MOTIFS** **What I’m Aiming For:** - **Themes:** - **The danger of stories**—**what happens when the narrative takes control?** - **The pressure to fit a trope**—**Mar doesn’t want to be the hero, but the world forces her to be.** - **The absurdity of existence**—**life is a badly written story, and we’re all just trying to survive it.** - **Motifs:** - **Emojis (🩸🩸🩸)**—**start as a mystery, become a warning, end as a threat.** - **Blood**—**not just gore, but **a metaphor for being "written into the story."** - **Bad writing**—**clichés, forced dialogue, deus ex machina moments that **fail spectacularly**.** - **Tea**—**Mar drinks it when she’s stressed, but it **never actually helps**.** **What I’m Avoiding:** - **Overly heavy themes**—**this is a dark comedy, not a philosophical treatise**. - **Forced symbolism**—**let the motifs emerge naturally**. - **A "message" about "sticking to the script"**—**the story should **resist easy morals**. --- #### **5. STYLISTIC CHOICES** **What I’m Aiming For:** - **Chapter Openings:** - **Start with **action, dialogue, or a **sharp observation** (no slow builds).** - **Example:** > *"The text came at 12:03 AM. Three blood emojis, no sender. I should’ve known then that my life was about to turn into a **bad fanfic**."* - **Dialogue:** - **British, snappy, **full of interruptions**. - **Example:** > *"You can’t just **yeet a chair at a ghost**, Jazz," I said. > "Watch me," he said, and **yeeted a chair at the ghost**. It worked. **Barely.**"* - **Descriptions:** - **Vivid but **not overly detailed**—**let the reader’s imagination fill in the gaps**. - **Example (horror):** > *"The mannequins weren’t moving. Not **yet**. But their **heads were turned just a little too far**, and their **smiles were just a little too wide**, like they’d been **stretched by something unseen**."* - **Example (comedy):** > *"Jazz’s **tinfoil hat** was **slipping**. Not because it was **badly made** (it was **very** badly made), but because he **kept scratching his head** like a **confused golden retriever**."* - **Pacing:** - **Short chapters, **quick cuts**—**keep the momentum high**. - **Cliffhangers at the end of each chapter** (but **not cheesy ones**). **What I’m Avoiding:** - **Over-describing settings**—**the world should feel **lived-in, not painted**. - **Long internal monologues**—**Mar’s thoughts should be **sharp and to the point**. - **Too much exposition in dialogue**—**let the action **reveal the world**. --- #### **6. ENDING INTENTIONS** **What I’m Aiming For:** - **A bittersweet, open-ended conclusion**—**Mar "wins," but nothing is really fixed**. - **A final twist that **undermines the victory** (the **new emoji text** implies **the cycle continues**). - **A last line that **feels like a **middle finger to the reader** (but in a **funny way**). - **Example:** > *"I deleted the text. Then I **blocked the number**. Then I **realised the number didn’t exist**. Somewhere, a **narrator screamed**. I **made myself a cup of tea** and **tried to forget**."* - **Leaving room for a sequel (but not **promising one**)—**the ambiguity should **feel intentional, not lazy**. **What I’m Avoiding:** - **A neat, happy ending**—**this isn’t a **feel-good story**. - **A post-credits tease**—**the ending should **stand on its own**. - **Over-explaining the supernatural**—**let the mystery linger**. --- # **CHAPTER PLANNING** ### **Chapter 1: "Blood in the Group Chat"** **Intentions:** - **Establish Mar’s voice, the mystery, and the tone.** - **Introduce the emoji text as **eerie but not yet supernatural**.** - **Set up the **rules of the world** (Mar’s blog, her dad’s conspiracy theories, the town’s weirdness).** **Key Scenes:** 1. **Opening:** Mar **live-debunking a "haunted" McDonald’s** (the "ghost" is a **raccoon with a flashlight**). - **Dialogue:** *"Ladies and gentlemen, **your ghost is a trash panda with a PhD in petty theft**."* 2. **The Text Arrives:** **🩸🩸🩸** at **12:03 AM**. - **Mar’s reaction:** *"Brilliant. **Either my phone’s possessed, or someone’s taking the piss**."* 3. **The Next Morning:** A **local teen is found dead** in the woods, **three slash marks on their wrist**. - **Dad’s reaction:** *"**Told you so!** The **Red Trio** are back!"* 4. **The Asylum Visit:** Mar and her dad **break in**, find **weird emoji graffiti**. - **Comedy beat:** Mar **trips over a corpse** (it’s a **mannequin**—her dad **screams like a banshee**). 5. **Cliffhanger:** As they leave, Mar **swears she sees a figure** in the window—**holding a phone with a glowing screen**. **Tone:** **Darkly comic, **mystery-driven**, **setting up the rules**. --- ### **Chapter 2: "The Red Trio (Probably)"** **Intentions:** - **Introduce the supernatural **as a bureaucracy, not a curse**.** - **Establish the Red Trio as **failed YA tropes**.** - **Mar starts **investigating for real**, but **nothing makes sense**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **Research Montage:** Mar and Lena **dig into urban legends** about the **Red Trio**. - **Theories:** - **Ghosts of a murdered teen trio (1998).** - **A glitch in reality (Jazz’s **tinfoil hat theory**).** - **A really bad AR filter (Eli’s **deadpan suggestion**).** 2. **Mr. Pemberton Appears:** A **bored Case Worker** for the supernatural. - **Dialogue:** > *"You’ve got a **narrative anomaly** on your hands. **Not ghosts. Not demons. Just **bad writing**."* 3. **The Warning:** *"If you keep poking at this, **you’ll become part of the story**. And **no one wants that**."* 4. **Cliffhanger:** Mar’s **phone rings—unknown number**. When she answers, she hears **her mother’s voice**: *"**Run**."* **Tone:** **Absurdist horror-comedy, **supernatural bureaucracy**. --- ### **Chapter 3: "The Asylum (Spoiler: It’s Worse Inside)"** **Intentions:** - **The team **breaks into the asylum**, finds **clues about the Red Trio**.** - **The supernatural **escalates—mannequins come to life**.** - **Mar finds her mum’s journal—**hints at the **truth about the emojis**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **The Break-In:** Mar, Lena, and Jazz **sneak in** (Jazz **complains the whole time**). 2. **The Emoji Room:** Walls **covered in 🩸🩸🩸**. 3. **Dr. Vexley’s Journal:** Last entry: *"**They’re not ghosts. They’re editors**."* 4. **The Mannequins:** They **come to life**, chase the group. - **Comedy beat:** Jazz **trips, knocks one over—it’s a kid in a bad costume**. 5. **Cliffhanger:** Mar’s **phone rings—her dad, screaming**: *"**They took Lena!**"* **Tone:** **Gothic horror meets teen slasher, **darkly funny**. --- ### **Chapter 4: "The Chosen One (No, Actually, It’s Worse)"** **Intentions:** - **Lena is kidnapped—**pulled into a **YA novel set**.** - **The Red Trio are revealed as **escaped tropes**.** - **Mar realises her mum was **trying to stop them**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **The "Safe House":** A **glitchy, VHS-style void** where **Lena is trapped in a "perfect YA story."** 2. **The Red Trio’s Backstories:** - **The Chosen One** (dramatic, **bad at being heroic**). - **The Mysterious Stranger** (keeps **forgetting his lines**). - **The Dead Best Friend** (not dead, just **really annoying**). 3. **Their Goal:** Turn the town into a **perfect YA novel**—**erase anyone who doesn’t fit**. 4. **Cliffhanger:** **Eli appears**, sipping tea: *"Oh, you’ve met the **Narrative Police**? **Yeah, they’re wankers**."* **Tone:** **Meta-YA satire, **absurdist villain reveal**. --- ### **Chapter 5: "The Rules of the Game (We’re All Just Characters)"** **Intentions:** - **Eli explains the **rules of the supernatural world**.** - **Mar and the team **infiltrate the Red Trio’s "control room."** - **The horror of being **trapped in a story**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **Eli’s Reveal:** He’s from a **place where stories are real**. 2. **The Rules:** - **You can’t kill them (they’re part of the story).** - **You can’t outrun them (they rewrite reality).** - **The only way to win is to **break the fourth wall**.** 3. **The Control Room:** A **glitchy void** where the Red Trio **edit reality**. 4. **Cliffhanger:** Mar **finds her mum’s file**—before she can read it, **the Dead Best Friend grabs her** and **drags her into the story**. **Tone:** **Surreal, *Fight Club*-meets-*Westworld*, **darkly funny**. --- ### **Chapter 6: "Marnie Vexley, The Reluctant Hero (Ugh, Fine)"** **Intentions:** - **Mar is trapped in a **bad YA novel**.** - **Her mum is the "villain"**—but she’s **trying to burn the system down**. - **The horror of **being a character in a story**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **The YA Novel Set:** **Clichéd dialogue, forced character growth, a love triangle (Jazz vs. Eli).** 2. **Mar’s Mum:** *"**The only way out is to become the author**."* 3. **Dark Moment:** Mar **realises her choices aren’t real**—she’s **just following a script**. 4. **Comedy Beat:** She **tries to skip the "emotional breakdown" scene**—the **narrator forces her to monologue**. 5. **Cliffhanger:** Her **mum whispers**: *"**You have to rewrite the ending**."* **Tone:** **Existential horror-comedy, **deconstruction of YA tropes**. --- ### **Chapter 7: "The Final (Badly Written) Boss Fight"** **Intentions:** - **Mar "rewrites" herself**—**refuses to follow the script**. - **The Red Trio panic** because **she’s breaking the rules**. - **A climax that’s a **deconstruction of YA tropes**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **Mar’s Rebellion:** She **yeets a book at the Chosen One’s head**. 2. **The Mysterious Stranger tries to monologue**—Mar **records it and posts it online** (he **dies of embarrassment**). 3. **The Dead Best Friend screams about betrayal**—Mar **points out he was never her friend** (he **poofs into nothing**). 4. **Comedy Beat:** The **narrator keeps trying to add tension**—Mar **keeps interrupting**: *"**No, I’m not doing that. That’s a bad trope**."* 5. **Cliffhanger:** The **world starts glitching**—**reality is unravelling**. **Tone:** **Action-comedy with a side of anarchy, **meta-YA satire**. --- ### **Chapter 8: "The End (Or Is It? Probably Not.)"** **Intentions:** - **The Red Trio are gone, but the world is **broken**.** - **Mar’s mum is back—but **different**.** - **A final twist that **undermines the victory**.** **Key Scenes:** 1. **The Aftermath:** - **Lena is fine (but thinks she’s in a simulation).** - **Jazz is weirdly chill (suspicious).** - **Eli is gone.** 2. **Mar’s Mum:** She’s **colder, more distant**. *"**You fixed it. But nothing’s ever really fixed**."* 3. **Final Twist:** Mar **checks her phone**—**a new text**: > **🩸🩸🩸** > **From: UNKNOWN** > **Message: "See you in the next chapter."** 4. **Last Line (Darkly Comic):** > *"I deleted the text. Then I **blocked the number**. Then I **realised the number didn’t exist**. Somewhere, a **narrator screamed**. I **made myself a cup of tea** and **tried to forget**."* **Tone:** **Bittersweet, cyclical, **with a wink to the audience**. --- **Final Notes:** - **Each chapter should **end on a cliffhanger or a **darkly funny beat**.** - **The supernatural should **feel glitchy and unresolved**—**not a neat mystery, but a **messy, absurd nightmare**.** - **Mar’s voice should **carry the tone**—**sarcastic, exhausted, but **secretly hopeful**.** **Next Steps:** 1. **Write Chapter 1 as a **test run**—see if the **voice and tone** work. 2. **Flesh out the Red Trio’s **dialogue and backstories**. 3. **Plan the **YA novel sequence** (Chapter 6) **carefully**—it’s the **most meta and risky part**. 4. **Keep the **pacing tight**—**no slow moments, only **escalating weirdness**.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# **Making It More Human: A Critique of LLM Writing Traps & How to Avoid Them in *🩸🩸🩸*** Large Language Models (LLMs) are incredible tools for generating **coherent, structurally sound, and stylistically consistent** writing—but they also have **tell-tale weaknesses** that can make prose feel **sterile, over-polished, or fundamentally *inhuman***. Given the **darkly comic, British, YA, and meta-fictional** nature of *🩸🩸🩸*, these pitfalls are **especially dangerous** because they risk **undermining the story’s rawness, humour, and emotional authenticity**. Below, I’ll: 1. **Break down common LLM writing traps** (and why they’re problematic for this story). 2. **Critique the current plan** for falling into these traps. 3. **Brainstorm humanising strategies** to make the writing feel **messier, funnier, and more real**. --- ## **PART 1: COMMON LLM WRITING TRAPS (AND WHY THEY’RE BAD FOR THIS STORY)** ### **1. The "Safe Middle" Problem** **What it is:** LLMs **avoid extremes**—they **soften edges**, **moderate tones**, and **seek consensus** in storytelling. This leads to: - **Predictable character arcs** (e.g., the reluctant hero **always** becomes the hero). - **Neutral, inoffensive humour** (jokes that **won’t offend anyone**, so they **don’t land hard**). - **Ambiguity without risk** (open endings that **feel deliberate but don’t *mean* anything**). **Why it’s bad for *🩸🩸🩸*:** - This story **needs to be *uncomfortable***—it’s about **a world that doesn’t make sense**, a protagonist who **hates being special**, and a villain that’s **a parody of YA tropes**. - **Safe writing would:** - Make Mar **too likable** (she needs to be **flawed, exhausted, and sometimes unlikeable**). - Softened the **dark humour** (e.g., **jokes about dead bodies** should **land with a thud, not a chuckle**). - Given the ending **a false sense of closure** (it should **feel unfinished, not *artfully* ambiguous**). **Example of LLM Safe Writing (Bad for This Story):** > *"Marnie sighed, her heart heavy with the weight of responsibility. She knew this was her moment—her chance to step up and save the day, even if she didn’t want to. After all, heroes were made in moments like these, weren’t they?"* **Why it’s bad:** - **Too sentimental.** - **Assumes Mar *will* be the hero** (she **won’t**, and that’s the point). - **Feels like a **self-help book**, not a **darkly comic YA horror story**. --- ### **2. The "Neat Bow" Problem** **What it is:** LLMs **love resolution**. They **hate loose ends** because they **disrupt the illusion of a "complete" story**. This leads to: - **Forced character reconciliations** (e.g., **the villain gets a sad backstory**, the hero **forgives them**). - **Over-explained supernatural rules** (because the LLM **feels the need to "make sense" of everything**). - **False symmetry** (e.g., **the story starts and ends in the same place**, but **nothing has really changed**). **Why it’s bad for *🩸🩸🩸*:** - This story **should *not* make sense**. The **supernatural should feel glitchy**, the **villains should be ridiculous**, and the **ending should feel *wrong***. - **Neat bows would:** - **Explain the Red Trio too clearly** (they should **remain a mystery**). - **Give Mar a "hero’s journey" arc** (she **doesn’t want one**). - **Make the emoji text *mean something*** (it **should just be creepy**). **Example of LLM Neat Bow Writing (Bad for This Story):** > *"As the last of the Red Trio vanished into the void, Marnie finally understood—the emojis had been a cry for help, a way for the lost souls to communicate across dimensions. She smiled, knowing she had done the right thing, even if the world would never know her sacrifice."* **Why it’s bad:** - **Over-explains the emojis** (they **don’t need to mean anything**). - **Gives Mar a "hero’s journey" payoff** (she **would hate this**). - **Feels like a **Hallmark card**, not a **darkly comic horror story**. --- ### **3. The "Impressive Prose" Problem** **What it is:** LLMs **sometimes overwrite**—they **try too hard to sound "literary" or "clever"**, leading to: - **Purple prose** (overly flowery descriptions that **draw attention to themselves**). - **Overly clever metaphors** (that **don’t actually *mean* anything**). - **Unnatural dialogue** (characters **sound like they’re in a play**, not real life). **Why it’s bad for *🩸🩸🩸*:** - This story **needs to feel *real***—**messy, funny, and sometimes awkward**. - **Impressive prose would:** - **Make the dark humour feel forced** (e.g., **jokes that are *trying* too hard to be edgy**). - **Slow down the pacing** (with **overly descriptive set pieces**). - **Make the characters sound **unlike real teens** (who **don’t talk in monologues**). **Example of LLM Impressive Prose (Bad for This Story):** > *"The asylum loomed before her, a monolith of decaying grandeur, its broken windows like the hollow eyes of a long-dead god, weeping tears of shattered glass into the abyss below."* **Why it’s bad:** - **Too poetic for a **darkly comic YA story**. - **Slows the pacing** (this should be **a quick, eerie observation**, not a **paragraph of gothic description**). - **Feels like a **horror novel**, not a **teen’s blog post about a haunting**. --- ### **4. The "Avoiding Conflict" Problem** **What it is:** LLMs **hate conflict**—they **smooth over arguments**, **avoid awkward silences**, and **make characters get along too easily**. This leads to: - **Characters who **never fight** (even when they should). - **Dialogue that’s **too polite** (teens **don’t talk like adults**). - **Problems that **resolve too neatly** (because the LLM **wants everyone to be happy**). **Why it’s bad for *🩸🩸🩸*:** - This story **needs friction**—**Mar should clash with Jazz, doubt Lena, and hate her dad’s conspiracy theories**. - **Avoiding conflict would:** - **Make the dynamic between Mar and Jazz **too friendly** (they should **bicker constantly**). - **Prevent Mar from **doubting herself** (she should **question everything**). - **Make the supernatural feel **too easy to solve** (it **should be confusing and frustrating**). **Example of LLM Avoiding Conflict (Bad for This Story):** > *"‘I just don’t understand why you’re so against this,’ Jazz said gently. ‘We’re all in this together, Marnie. We should trust each other.’ > Marnie sighed, her anger melting away. ‘You’re right, Jazz. I’m sorry. Let’s work as a team.’"* **Why it’s bad:** - **Too mature for teens** (they **wouldn’t resolve it this easily**). - **Kills the tension** (they should **argue, not hug it out**). - **Feels like a **after-school special**, not a **darkly comic horror story**. --- ### **5. The "Over-Explanation" Problem** **What it is:** LLMs **love to explain things**—they **feel the need to "make sense" of the world**, leading to: - **Info-dumps** (characters **suddenly knowing too much**). - **Supernatural rules that are **too clear** (which **ruins the mystery**). - **Backstories that **don’t earn their weight** (e.g., **a villain’s tragic past** that **doesn’t affect the plot**). **Why it’s bad for *🩸🩸🩸*:** - This story **should *not* make sense**. The **supernatural should feel glitchy**, the **villains should be ridiculous**, and the **rules should be unclear**. - **Over-explanation would:** - **Ruins the mystery of the emojis** (they **should just be creepy**). - **Make the Red Trio **too sympathetic** (they should **remain annoying and vague**). - **Give Mar’s mum a **dramatic backstory** that **doesn’t matter** (her disappearance should **stay mysterious**). **Example of LLM Over-Explanation (Bad for This Story):** > *"The Red Trio weren’t just ghosts—they were fragments of a failed experiment in dimensional travel, conducted by a rogue scientist in the 1990s. Their emoji markings were a subconscious cry for help, a way to communicate across the veil between worlds. Marnie now understood why they had chosen her—her mother’s research had accidentally opened a portal, and the Trio saw her as their only link to the living world."* **Why it’s bad:** - **Over-explains the emojis** (they **don’t need to mean anything**). - **Gives the Red Trio a **dramatic backstory** (they should **remain annoying and vague**). - **Makes the story **feel like a **sci-fi lecture**, not a **darkly comic horror story**. --- ## **PART 2: CRITIQUE OF THE CURRENT PLAN (WHERE IT MIGHT FALL INTO LLM TRAPS)** ### **1. Marnie Vexley’s Character Arc (Risk: "Safe Middle" Hero’s Journey)** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The plan **risks making Mar too likable**—she’s **supposed to be exhausted, sarcastic, and sometimes unlikeable**, but the **current outline keeps her too competent**. - **Current weakness:** Her **refusal to be the hero** is **a great premise**, but the **plan doesn’t push her far enough into **self-sabotage or failure**. **Humanising Fixes:** ✅ **Make her *worse* at being a hero**—she **should fail spectacularly** at least once. ✅ **Give her a **flaw that actively hurts the plot** (e.g., **she refuses to believe in the supernatural until it’s almost too late**). ✅ **Let her **be mean to people** (e.g., **she snaps at Lena when she’s stressed**). ✅ **Make her **hate the idea of being special**—not just **complain about it**, but **actively reject help** when she needs it. **Example of a More Human Mar:** > *"‘We need a plan,’ Jazz said, rubbing his temples. > ‘No, *you* need a plan,’ Mar snapped. ‘I need a **bloody cigarette** and a **time machine** so I can **un-invent the internet**.’ > Lena shot her a look. ‘You don’t smoke.’ > ‘I **do now**.’"* --- ### **2. The Red Trio (Risk: "Over-Explanation" & "Neat Bow")** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The **current plan risks making the Red Trio **too sympathetic** (their **backstories are too clear**, their **motivations are too logical**). - The **ending risks giving them a **false sense of resolution** (the **new emoji text** is a **good twist**, but the **plan doesn’t push far enough into **absurdity**). **Humanising Fixes:** ✅ **Make them **more annoying than scary**—they should **feel like **bad fanfiction come to life** (e.g., **the Chosen One keeps **forgetting his own backstory**). ✅ **Give them **no real motive**—they’re **not villains, they’re **glitches in the system** (like **a **buffering YouTube video** that **won’t stop playing**). ✅ **Make their "defeat" **ridiculous**—e.g., **Mar doesn’t "beat" them, she **accidentally unmutes them** and they **get stuck in an infinite loop of their own bad dialogue**. ✅ **End on a **note of absurdity**—e.g., **the last thing we see is the Chosen One **still monologuing** as the screen **glitches to static**. **Example of a More Human Red Trio:** > *The Chosen One adjusted his **leather jacket** (which was **clearly from Primark**) and **cleared his throat**. > *"You **fools**! You **cannot defeat the power of destiny**!" he declared. > Jazz **yawned**. *"Mate, your **destiny** is **a **participation trophy** from **2008**."* > The Chosen One **paused**. *"…What?"* > *"Your **backstory** is **a **Wikipedia page** someone **wrote at 3 AM**."* > The Chosen One **frowned**. *"…I **do** remember a **Wikipedia page**…"* --- ### **3. The Supernatural Rules (Risk: "Over-Explanation")** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The **current plan risks **explaining too much**—e.g., **Mr. Pemberton’s bureaucracy**, **Eli’s origins**, **the "rules" of the story**. - The **supernatural should feel **glitchy and unresolved**, not **like a **D&D campaign**. **Humanising Fixes:** ✅ **Let the rules **contradict themselves**—e.g., **one chapter says you can’t kill the Red Trio, the next says you can, but it **doesn’t work**). ✅ **Make the "explanations" **feel like **bad fanfiction**—e.g., **Eli’s backstory keeps changing** (is he **a time traveller? A **failed protagonist? A **dream**?). ✅ **Let the characters **argue about the rules**—e.g., **Mar and Jazz **debate whether the emojis are **a curse or a **glitch**, but **neither is right**. ✅ **End with **more questions than answers**—e.g., **the final text (🩸🩸🩸) **could be a **prank, a **warning, or a **glitch**—**we never know**. **Example of a More Human Supernatural System:** > *"So **what are they, then?** Jazz demanded. **Ghosts? Demons? **A **really bad **AR filter**?" > Eli **sipped his tea**. *"They’re **a **narrative anomaly**."* > *"That’s **not a **real answer**," Mar groaned. > *"It’s **the only answer**," Eli said. *"The **real question** is **why you’re **still here**."* > *"Because **I haven’t **bloody died** yet!"* Mar snapped. > Eli **smiled**. *"**Not yet**."* --- ### **4. The Ending (Risk: "Neat Bow" & "Avoiding Conflict")** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The **current ending risks feeling **too neat**—the **new emoji text is a good twist**, but the **rest of the resolution feels **too tidy**. - The **characters **shouldn’t all be **okay**—someone **should be messed up**, someone **should be missing**, someone **should be **lying**. **Humanising Fixes:** ✅ **Make the "victory" **hollow**—e.g., **the Red Trio are gone, but the world is **worse** (e.g., **people remember the haunting **differently**, **Lena thinks she’s in a **simulation**, **Jazz is **too calm**). ✅ **Leave a **real, emotional wound**—e.g., **Mar’s mum is back, but she’s **not the same** (she **doesn’t remember her**, or she’s **too cold**). ✅ **End on a **note of **real uncertainty**—e.g., **the last line shouldn’t **wrap things up**, it should **make the reader **uneasy**. ✅ **Let the **humour be **dark, not **cute**—e.g., **the last joke shouldn’t **lighten the mood**, it should **make it **darker**. **Example of a More Human Ending:** > *Mar’s mum **sipped her tea**, her eyes **empty**. > *"You **did good**," she said. > *"You **don’t remember me**," Mar said. > Her mum **smiled**. *"I **remember enough**."* > Mar’s phone **buzzed**. **🩸🩸🩸**. > She **deleted it**. > Then she **blocked the number**. > Then she **realised the number **didn’t exist**. > Somewhere, a **narrator screamed**. > Mar **made herself another cup of tea**. > It **tasted like **ash**. --- ### **5. The Dialogue (Risk: "Impressive Prose" & "Avoiding Conflict")** **Potential LLM Trap:** - The **current plan risks making the dialogue **too polished**—teens **don’t talk like **Shakespearean monologuers**. - The **conflicts should be **messier**—**arguments should **derail**, **jokes should **fall flat**, **emotions should **spill over**. **Humanising Fixes:** ✅ **Make the dialogue **awkward**—e.g., **characters **interrupt**, **change subjects abruptly**, **say the wrong thing**. ✅ **Let the humour **fail sometimes**—e.g., **a joke **lands badly**, **a sarcastic remark **hits too close to home**. ✅ **Give characters **verbal tics**—e.g., **Jazz **rambles when nervous**, **Lena **cuts people off**, **Eli **speaks in riddles**. ✅ **Let the **supernatural **interrupt conversations**—e.g., **a **glitch in reality** **cuts off a **serious moment**. **Example of More Human Dialogue:** > *"You **can’t just **yeet a chair at a ghost**, Jazz," Mar said. > *"**Watch me**," he said, and **yeeted a chair**. > It **missed**. > The ghost **laughed**. > *"**That’s **not how **this works**," Lena sighed. > *"**How **do you **even know**?" Jazz snapped. > *"**Because I **read a **bloody book** once!"* > The ghost **cleared its throat**. > *"**Can we **focus**?" it said. > *"**No**," Mar said. *"**I’m **done**."* > She **walked out**. > The ghost **followed her**. > *"**You **can’t just **leave**!"* > *"**Watch me**," she said, and **slammed the door**. > The ghost **screamed**. > Jazz **grinned**. *"**That **went well**."* > Lena **facepalmed**. --- ## **PART 3: BRAINSTORMING HUMANISING STRATEGIES** ### **1. Make the Characters *Flawed, Not Just "Relatable"* **Goal:** **Teens are **messy, inconsistent, and sometimes **unlikeable**.** **Strategies:** - **Mar should **self-sabotage**—e.g., **she refuses help when she needs it**, **she **lies to her friends**, **she **takes her anger out on the wrong people**. - **Jazz should **be annoying, not just **smug**—e.g., **he **monopolises conversations**, **he **dismissive of Lena’s ideas**, **he **panics in ways that **make things worse**. - **Lena should **have a breaking point**—e.g., **she’s the **voice of reason**, but **she snaps when she’s pushed too far**. - **Eli should **be cryptic to the point of **annoying**—e.g., **he **won’t explain anything**, **he **disappears at bad times**, **he **seems to **know too much but **won’t say why**. **Example:** > *Mar **stared at the **emoji text** again. > *"**We need to **tell someone**," Lena said. > *"**No**," Mar said. > *"**Why **not**?"* > *"**Because **I don’t **trust anyone**."* > *"**Not even **me**?"* > Mar **didn’t answer**. > Lena **sighed**. *"**You’re **impossible**."* > *"**I know**," Mar said. *"**That’s **why I’m **still alive**."* --- ### **2. Make the Supernatural *Glitchy, Not Just "Scary"* **Goal:** **The supernatural should feel **unreliable, **confusing, and **sometimes **ridiculous**.** **Strategies:** - **The rules should **change**—e.g., **one chapter says you can’t kill the Red Trio, the next says you can, but it **doesn’t work**. - **The emojis should **mean different things**—e.g., **first they’re a warning, then a **prank, then a **glitch**. - **The "explanations" should **contradict**—e.g., **Mr. Pemberton says one thing, Eli says another, and **neither is right**. - **Reality should **glitch in weird ways**—e.g., **time skips, people remember things wrong, **objects move on their own**. **Example:** > *"**So **what are they, then?** Jazz demanded. **Ghosts? **Demons? **A **really bad **AR filter**?" > *"**They’re **a **narrative anomaly**," Eli said. > *"**That’s **not a **real answer**," Mar groaned. > *"**It’s **the only answer**," Eli said. *"**The **real question** is **why you’re **still here**."* > *"**Because **I haven’t **bloody died** yet!"* Mar snapped. > Eli **smiled**. *"**Not yet**."* > The **lights flickered**. > When they **came back on**, Eli was **gone**. > *"**Where’d **he go**?" Jazz said. > *"**I don’t **know**," Mar said. > *"**You **don’t **seem **surprised**," Lena said. > Mar **shrugged**. *"**I’m **used to it**."* > The **emoji text **buzzed again**. > **🩸🩸🩸**. --- ### **3. Make the Humour *Dark, Not Just "Funny"* **Goal:** **The jokes should **land hard**, **not just **make the reader laugh**.** **Strategies:** - **Jokes should **come from **real frustration**—e.g., **Mar’s sarcasm should **cut deep**, **Jazz’s rambling should **annoy people**. - **The humour should **be uneven**—e.g., **some jokes should **fall flat**, **some should **hit too hard**. - **The darkest jokes should **come from **the darkest moments**—e.g., **laughing while running from a corpse**. - **The humour should **reflect the characters**—e.g., **Mar’s jokes are **sarcastic**, **Jazz’s are **smug**, **Lena’s are **dry**. **Example:** > *The **corpse** was **still warm**. > *"**Brilliant**," Mar said. *"**Now I’ve got to **call the police** *and* **my dad**."* > *"**At least it’s **not your **homework**," Jazz said. > Mar **glared at him**. > *"**Too soon**?" he said. > *"**Yes**," Lena said. > *"**I was **joking**," Jazz muttered. > *"**We know**," Mar said. *"**That’s **the problem**."* --- ### **4.
